# Overarching Reasearch Question
## Do the optional tutoring sessions benefit students who have no coding experience? If they do, then how can we optimize these tutoring sessions?

# Data
We got our data from a survey in Izzy Hink's two sections of COMP 110. COMP 110 is an introductory coding class so there is a large majority of students who have never coded before. The measures we used to determine if the tutoring sessions were successful were Understanding, prior_exp, and tutoring_effective. The survey had many other questions so we had to cleane the data, filtering out all of the unwanted measueres and getting rid of values that were empty. The empty values were in the tutoring_effective measure because not everyone in the two sections of COMP 110 went to tutoring. 

# Data Analysis
## Graph 1, Histogram 1
Our first graph is a histogram which counts students' rating for the tutoring_effective measure. Both total students and students with no expreince are plotted. 

<img src="static/imgs/First Histogram.png" alt="Histogram 1."  width="500">



This graph shows us that out of everyone in the class who went to tutoring, most of them rated the effectiveness either a 4, 5, 6, or 7. A very small population rated it a 1, 2, or 3. The only people to rate it a 1 had experinece before. Students overall belive that tutoring is effecctive. The total number of no experince students who went to tutoring outweighs the expereinced students who went to tutoring, revealing that tutoring should be directed towards no experience students.

## Graph 2, Histogram 2
The second graph is a histogram with the same metrics as the first but instead of a count, there is a percentage.


<img src="static/imgs/Second Histogram.png" alt="Histogram 2."  width="500">

This graph allows us to determine if a higher percentage of no experience students belive that tutoring is effective compared to the total students. The graph shows that a higher percentage of no expereince students think that tutoring is effective compared to the total, further revealing the importance of tutoring. 

## Graph 3, Heatmap 
The the third graph is a heatmap comparing the Understanding and tutoring metric. 

<img src="static/imgs/Heatmap COMP 110.png" alt="Heatmap."  width="500">


This graph does not seem to reveal any trend between the two metrics. This is likely due to the survey being a self assessment rather than a definite statistic. 


# Conclusion

We recommend that COMP 110 increases the amount of tutoring hours because the data shows that students with none to less than one month of coding experience believe that they are beneficial. As an introductory computer science course, the team behind COMP 110 should advocate for people with no coding experience the most. When considering the people who went to the tutoring sessions, the majority of the responses claimed that they thought the tutoring sessions were 5, 6 or 7 in effectiveness. Now, when considering people who have little to none coding experience and comparing it to the entire class's perspective on tutoring sessions, there is a slight increase in the percentage of non coding student's belief in tutoring efficiency. When looking at tutoring effective vs understanding in all students that attended tutoring sessions, there doesn't seem to be a correlation. Despite this, tutoring is still effective since the survey was based on what students themselves thought rather than a more accurate measure like quiz scores.
If we have more tutoring sessions available, then more students can participate and eventually do better in the class. While this would create more work for TAs, it would overall benefit the students. Despite a lack of a correlation of students who think tutoring is helpful and how well they think they understand the content, we should still increase the amount of tutoring hours based on the fact that a majority of the population believes they are beneficial.
